sections.intro: Start with the browser's styling pipeline and the cascade rules that decide which declarations win.

blocks.title: Cascade mental model
blocks.body: Map origin, importance, layer, specificity, and source order before writing selectors.

sections.intro: Use selectors precisely so styles stay expressive without becoming fragile.

blocks.title: Selector toolkit
blocks.body: Cover combinators, attribute selectors, pseudo-classes, and pseudo-elements in one pass.

sections.intro: Control reuse and override behavior with specificity, inheritance, custom properties, layers, and scope.

blocks.title: Token flow
blocks.body: Define how values inherit, cascade, and override across nested UI boundaries.

sections.intro: Build strong layout intuition around the box model, sizing rules, overflow, and positioning.

blocks.title: Spatial primitives
blocks.body: Keep the chapter scaffold focused on the geometry that every layout depends on.

sections.intro: Use Flexbox for one-dimensional distribution and alignment.

blocks.title: Flexible rows and columns
blocks.body: Describe how the main axis and cross axis shape everyday UI composition.

sections.intro: Use Grid for explicit two-dimensional page and component layout.

blocks.title: Track-based layout
blocks.body: Introduce rows, columns, placement, and subgrid without adding legacy cards.

sections.intro: Make layouts adapt across viewport sizes, writing modes, and component containers.

blocks.title: Responsive strategy
blocks.body: Reserve media queries, logical properties, and container queries for the skeleton stage.

sections.intro: Shape the visual language with typography, color systems, backgrounds, and effects.

blocks.title: Visual styling basics
blocks.body: Leave room for deeper color and type systems while keeping the chapter navigable.

sections.intro: Add motion thoughtfully with transforms, transitions, animations, and motion tokens.

blocks.title: Motion foundations
blocks.body: Keep the motion chapter as a roadmap for later examples and patterns.

sections.intro: Organize CSS for scale using resets, tokens, layers, and system-level conventions.

blocks.title: System architecture
blocks.body: Reserve space for resets, token design, and scalable conventions.

sections.intro: Understand rendering cost so we can make CSS faster without premature micro-optimization.

blocks.title: Performance model
blocks.body: Introduce layout, paint, and composite cost as the basis for later tuning.

sections.intro: Make CSS resilient for keyboard users, assistive tech, and user preference settings.

blocks.title: Resilient defaults
blocks.body: Keep accessibility and preference-aware styling visible from the start.

sections.intro: Explore newer platform CSS capabilities that reduce JavaScript and integrate with native UI primitives.

blocks.title: Platform features
blocks.body: Hold space for anchor positioning, popover-adjacent CSS, and other platform features.

sections.intro: Close with trade-offs, debugging instincts, and interview-ready recall for the CSS ecosystem.

blocks.title: Capstone interview bank
blocks.body: Keep a representative slice of the legacy Q&A here while the skeleton is still being rebuilt.

qa.question: What is the cascade in CSS?
qa.answerHtml: The cascade decides which declaration wins by considering importance, origin, layers, specificity, and source order.

qa.question: What is the difference between display: none and visibility: hidden?
qa.answerHtml: display: none removes the element from layout, while visibility: hidden keeps its space but hides it visually.

qa.question: What is the box model?
qa.answerHtml: The box model is content, padding, border, and margin.

qa.question: What is the difference between margin and padding?
qa.answerHtml: Padding sits inside the border and adds space within the element; margin sits outside the border and adds space between elements.

qa.question: What is the difference between Flexbox and Grid?
qa.answerHtml: Flexbox is primarily one-dimensional, while Grid is two-dimensional.

qa.question: What are pseudo-classes?
qa.answerHtml: Pseudo-classes target elements based on state or position, such as :hover, :focus, and :nth-child().

qa.question: What are logical properties?
qa.answerHtml: Logical properties adapt to writing mode and direction, using inline and block concepts instead of physical sides.

qa.question: What is a Block Formatting Context (BFC)?
qa.answerHtml: A BFC isolates layout behavior so floats are contained and margin-collapsing is constrained.

qa.question: What is the gap property?
qa.answerHtml: gap controls spacing between grid or flex items without adding margins to the children.

qa.question: How do CSS animations perform vs JavaScript animations?
qa.answerHtml: CSS transforms and opacity can stay on the compositor, while JavaScript often competes for the main thread.

qa.question: What is subgrid?
qa.answerHtml: subgrid lets a child grid inherit track sizing from its parent grid.

qa.question: What is CSS containment?
qa.answerHtml: Containment limits how much of the page the browser must consider when an element changes.
